bibliographic_data.title: Adversarial Memory Networks for Action Prediction
bibliographic_data.abstract: Action prediction aims to infer the forthcoming human action with partially-observed videos, which is a challenging task due to the limited information underlying early observations. Existing methods mainly adopt a reconstruction strategy to handle this task, expecting to learn a single mapping function from partial observations to full videos to facilitate the prediction process. In this study, we propose adversarial memory networks (AMemNet) to generate the “full video” feature conditioning on a partial video query from two new aspects.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes adversarial memory networks (AMemNet) to generate full-video features conditioning on partial video queries, addressing the challenges of limited information in early observations.
core_content.key_methodology: AMemNet uses a key-value structured memory generator and a class-aware discriminator. The memory generator memorizes different partial videos as key memories and dynamically writes full videos in value memories with gating mechanism and querying attention. The class-aware discriminator guides the memory generator to deliver realistic and discriminative full video features.
outcomes: Proposed AMemNet model achieves over 90% accuracy by observing only 10% of the beginning video frames on the UCF101 dataset.
Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of AMemNet compared with state-of-the-art methods.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Action prediction is a critical research topic that can be applied in various real-world scenarios such as video surveillance, autonomous navigation, and human-computer interaction. Unlike action recognition, which recognizes the category of an action based on a complete video, action prediction aims to understand human activities at an early stage using partially-observed videos before the entire action execution. The key challenge is how to enhance discriminative information for partial videos. This paper proposes adversarial memory networks (AMemNet) to address this issue by generating full-video features from partial video queries. AMemNet uses a key-value structured memory generator and a class-aware discriminator, which together enable dynamic updating of video memories based on different queries. The model employs gating mechanisms and attention weights for memory writing and reading processes. Extensive experiments on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets show that AMemNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods with high accuracy.
